coredata.dc:title: Triggering dual-mode dynamic optical responses by anchoring carbon dots on MnO2 hollow spheres
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Sisi
Cai, Yajing
Situ, Jiaying
Wang, Biwen
Ying, Beichen
He, Jin
Liu, Zhao-Qing
Zhuang, Jianle
Chen, Yibo
coredata.dc:description: Multi-mode responsive optical materials with versatile application prospects for optical sensing, optoelectrical devices, and information encryption are highly desired. Here we integrate fluorescent carbon dots onto the hollow MnO2 spheres, and modulate the interaction between them to achieve a water-stimulated responsive system with dual-mode dynamic signals in both fluorescence and structural color. The composite film shows a structural color shifting from blue to green as well as displays a fluorescence “on” in the presence of trace water, with both changes capable of being reversibly switched up to 10 times. The amide bond between the two components enables the controllable dispersion and aggregation of the carbon dots which is essential to the reversibility. Fabrication of several security labels with separate information in structural color and fluorescence channels demonstrates the application potential of the composite. This work provides a concise route to develop new multi-mode responsive optical materials for applications in anti-counterfeiting, sensing, and coding.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water-stimulated response
Dual-mode
Heterointerfaces
Interaction manipulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for CDs/h-MnO2-1. TEM images of (b) SiO2, (c) h-MnO2, (d) CDs/h-MnO2-1 and (e) enlarged area in CDs/h-MnO2-1. (f) XRD patterns of CDs, h-MnO2, and CDs/h-MnO2-1. (g) Elemental mapping images of Mn, O, N, and C in CDs/h-MnO2-1. (h) Zeta potentials of CDs, h-MnO2, h-MnO2-A, and CDs/h-MnO2-1.

(a) TGA and DSC (inset) curves of CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2. (b) FTIR spectra of CDs, h-MnO2, h-MnO2-A, CDs/h-MnO2-1, and CDs/h-MnO2-2. (c) XPS survey spectra. (d–e) High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of O 1s (d) and N 1s (e) in CDs, h-MnO2-A, CDs/h-MnO2-1, and CDs/h-MnO2-2.

(a) Schematic diagram of the viewing angles, (b) the corresponding photographs, and (c) reflectance spectra with different viewing angles of CDs/h-MnO2-1 film. (d) Reflectance spectrum of CDs/h-MnO2-2 film when θ = 90°, insets are the images of the film and the CDs dispersion (1.4 mg/mL in water).

(a) Schematic illustration of water-triggered dual-mode response. (b) Reflectance spectra and (c) variations of CDs/h-MnO2-1 film when θ = 90°. Fluorescence spectra of (d) CDs/h-MnO2-1 and (e) CDs/h-MnO2-2 (λ ex = 365 nm). Fluorescence variations of (f) CDs/h-MnO2-1 and (g) CDs/h-MnO2-2 in 10 consecutive wet/dry cycles (λ ex = 365 nm).
(a) Formation scheme of the amide bonds. (b) Schematic illustration of the water response mechanism of CDs/h-MnO2-1 (up) and CDs/h-MnO2-2 (down) with or without amide bonds.
(a) Schematic diagram of water-triggered anti-counterfeiting patterns made with h-MnO2 and CDs/h-MnO2-1. (b) Photographs of decrypted and encrypted anti-counterfeiting patterns of “Swan”.

head.ce:title: Triggering dual-mode dynamic optical responses by anchoring carbon dots on MnO2 hollow spheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Device Physics of Complex Materials, Zernike Institute for Advanced Materials, University of Groningen, Groningen, the Netherlands
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Device Physics of Complex Materials, Zernike Institute for Advanced Materials, University of Groningen, Groningen, the Netherlands

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-mode responsive optical materials with versatile application prospects for optical sensing, optoelectrical devices, and information encryption are highly desired. Here we integrate fluorescent carbon dots onto the hollow MnO2 spheres, and modulate the interaction between them to achieve a water-stimulated responsive system with dual-mode dynamic signals in both fluorescence and structural color. The composite film shows a structural color shifting from blue to green as well as displays a fluorescence “on” in the presence of trace water, with both changes capable of being reversibly switched up to 10 times. The amide bond between the two components enables the controllable dispersion and aggregation of the carbon dots which is essential to the reversibility. Fabrication of several security labels with separate information in structural color and fluorescence channels demonstrates the application potential of the composite. This work provides a concise route to develop new multi-mode responsive optical materials for applications in anti-counterfeiting, sensing, and coding.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Responsive optical materials are attracting more and more attention due to their environment-dependent optical signals with strong anti-interference ability, fast response rate, and high sensitivity [1,2]. Given the simplicity and eco-compatibility [3,4], water-stimulated responsive materials stand out among the thermochromic [5,6], mechanochromic [7,8], solvatochromic [9,10], and photochromic [11,12] ones. Nowadays, widely studied water-stimulated responsive materials include organic molecules [13], covalent-organic frameworks [14], and metal halides [15–17], which exhibit color-changing or on–off fluorescent switching based on water-induced molecular self-assembly, ring-opening reactions, or structural transition. However, one single type of optical signal is far away from meeting the requirements of signal complexity which are highly desired in advanced anti-counterfeiting, optical data storage, and encryption. Currently, a prevalent approach to enhance the signal complexity is combining multiple luminescence responses, such as upconversion/downconversion luminescence, persistent luminescence, mechanoluminescence, etc [18–20]. Nevertheless, the implementation of this approach is often hindered by the reliance on multiple (laser) light sources and sophisticated optical instruments for signal triggering and detection, thereby limiting its widespread applicability.

Structural color is a coloration resulting from the interaction of light with micro- and nanostructures, featuring durability, environmental friendliness, and most importantly, independence from external devices [21,22]. It provides a highly promising pathway to improve the capacity and diversity of information storage, and several pioneer works have demonstrated the advantage of combining structural color with fluorescence [23–27]. For example, by introducing trace amounts of the highly efficient fluorescent material DC5 into cholesteric liquid crystals, Qin et al. enabled a complex to display both vibrant structural colors and bright fluorescence [26]. However, the composite system delivers only static information via two distinct channels without dynamic changes, highly limiting the information-carrying capacity. Alternatively, Cai et al developed a carbon dots-tagged responsive hydrogel using the assembled silica nanoparticles as the hard template [28]. The hydrogel exhibits dynamic color/fluorescence changes in response to different solvents. However, the synthesis process is intricate, involving multiple steps such as solvothermal treatment and the use of corrosive hydrofluoric acid to remove the silica template, which may raise concerns regarding scalability and environmental impact. Thus, composites with multiple dynamic optical responses and high manufacturing feasibility are highly desired but still rarely reported. In addition, there remains a lack of adequate understanding of the interactions between these multiple components that can be leveraged to optimize performance metrics.

Recently, we reported the hollow manganese dioxide (h-MnO2) nanospheres with size-dependent structural colors [29]. They exhibit reversible and rapid color-changing under exposure to water, with their internal cavities acting as water reservoirs. Carbon dots (CDs), with the advantage of affordability, high biocompatibility, and preparation simplicity, have an interesting water-induced fluorescence “on” phenomenon while showing quenching of the fluorescence in the aggregated state [30,31]. Herein, we prepared a water-triggered dual-mode dynamic response system in both structural color and fluorescence channels by coupling these two materials via amide bonds. By detailed surface, microstructure, and optical characterizations, the interactions between MnO2 and CDs were elucidated and the response mechanism was clarified. The CDs become dispersed under water treatment because the hydrogen bonds between CDs can be easily disrupted. Meanwhile, the dispersed CDs can still be anchored on the surface of the h-MnO2 via amide bonding to guarantee response reversibility. In contrast, the sample without amide bonding finally loses its fluorescence performance due to the escape of the CDs. Fabrication of several security labels with unique structural color and fluorescence patterns offers a platform for multi-information encoding, representing significant application potential in anti-counterfeiting and sensing fields.

All the raw chemical reagents were purchased and used directly without further purification. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, A.R.), ammonia solution (A.R., 25 %), formaldehyde solution (A.R.), KMnO4 (A.R.), urea (A.R.) were obtained from Guangzhou Chemicals. NaOH (A.R., 95%), 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, A.R., 99%) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Chemicals. Resorcinol (A.R., 99%), citric acid monohydrate (A.R., 99.5%), 1-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC, 99.5%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 99.5%), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 10000) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemicals.

Citric acid monohydrate (2.00 g) and urea (2.00 g) were dissolved in 40 mL pure water and ultrasonicated for 10 min until the solids were dissolved. After being transferred to a microwave oven (Power: 800 W), the mixture was heated for 9 min followed by natural cooling, and a black viscous gel was obtained. Then it was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 1 h. The product was dispersed in 100 mL pure water, and then centrifugated at 10000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was reserved and dialyzed three times (MWCO: 1000) to obtain the product.

The h-MnO2 were synthesized according to our previous work [29]. Silica (SiO2) templates were first synthesized using a Stöber method. In a typical synthesis, 5.0 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was mixed with 80.0 mL of ethanol, 3.0 mL of pure water, and 5.0 mL of ammonia solution, and then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h to produce SiO2 nanospheres with a diameter of ≈180 nm. To coat a resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin shell, 50.0 mg of SiO2 spheres modified with PVP, 28.0 mL of water, 20 mg of resorcinol, and 28 µL of formaldehyde solution were subsequently added into a flask and thoroughly mixed. Then, a polymerization reaction was initiated by adding 100 µL of 2.8% ammonia solution. The reaction mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 2 h, followed by maintaining it at 100 °C for an additional 2 h to condense the RF shell. The resulting SiO2@RF suspension was then mixed with 80 mg of KMnO4 at room temperature to obtain SiO2@MnO2 spheres using a surface-initiated redox route. The h-MnO2 nanospheres were obtained after being etched in NaOH solution at 70 °C for 3 h.

The h-MnO2 obtained previously was mixed with 16.0 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 1.0 mL of APTES, and the mixture was heated at 60 °C for 4 h. After that, the product was subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed twice with anhydrous ethanol to obtain amino-modified h-MnO2 (h-MnO2-A). To prepare the CDs/h-MnO2, 1.0 mL of CDs suspension (1.4 mg/mL) was mixed with 16.0 mL anhydrous ethanol, 1.0 mL of EDC (15.0 mg), and 1.0 mL of NHS (7.5 mg), which were then stirred for 30 min. Afterward, 5.0 mL of the h-MnO2-A suspension prepared earlier was added rapidly, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 3 h. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, the final product was collected and named CDs/h-MnO2-1. All steps were the same as described above without APTES, EDC, and NHS in the preparation of the control sample CDs/h-MnO2-2.

The films used for response investigations and anti-counterfeiting demos were fabricated using the drop-casting method. Dispersions of CDs/h-MnO2-1 or other samples (∼2.5 mg /mL in ethanol) were evenly deposited onto a glass substrate and subsequently heated in an oven to evaporate the ethanol.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a PANalytical PW3040/60 X-ray powder diffractometer. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100F) was utilized to observe the morphology and microstructure of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, America) was used to investigate the electronic states of the samples. The contact angles were measured on a Dataphysics-OCA20 angle measuring and contour analysis system (Germany). The functional groups of these prepared products were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, TENSOR II+Hyperion2000). The reflectance spectra were measured by a fiber optic spectrometer (Ocean Optics USB 2000+ ) with a halogen lamp as the light source. The fluorescence spectra were performed on a fiber optic spectrometer (Ocean Optics USB 2000+ ) with a 365 nm UV lamp as the light source. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, NETZSCH STA 449 F3) was carried out at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 under oxygen (20%) and argon (80%) atmosphere. The zeta potential data were recorded at a nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (Opptronix OPT-919SZ).

The preparation scheme of the composite named CDs/h-MnO2-1 is depicted in Fig. 1 a. In this process, APTES was utilized as an amino-coupling reagent to modify the amino group on the surface of h-MnO2 to form h-MnO2-A. These amino groups interacted with the carboxyl groups of CDs to anchor the CDs on the surface of the h-MnO2 hollow spheres. The EDC and NHS were used to activate the carboxyl groups. As shown in Fig. 1b–d, SiO2, h-MnO2, and CDs/h-MnO2-1 exhibit uniform spherical morphologies with average sizes of about 175, 200, and 230 nm, respectively. The TEM image of the CDs (Fig. S1) reveals an average particle size of approximately 8 nm. The localized TEM image confirms that the CDs have been successfully grafted onto the surface of h-MnO2 with a relatively uniform distribution (Fig. 1e). The XRD patterns of CDs, h-MnO2, CDs/h-MnO2-1, and CDs/h-MnO2-2 are shown in Fig. 1f and Fig. S2. The CDs exhibit a noticeably wide diffraction peak in the range of 23°∼26°, which suggests the presence of highly disordered amorphous carbon [32]; the h-MnO2 shows distinct diffraction peaks at 25.3°, 37.3°, and 66.2°, which correspond to the (002), (−111), and (−005) crystal planes of K-birnessite type manganese oxide (δ-MnO2, PDF#80–1098) [33]. A high-resolution TEM image (Fig. S3) reveals the closely adjacent CDs and h-MnO2, with the lattice spacing of 0.33 and 0.35 nm corresponding to the (002) plane of CDs and the (002) plane of h-MnO2, respectively. The elemental mapping images (Fig. 1g) illustrate the uniform distribution of Mn, O, N, and C elements without aggregations or deletions, suggesting a homogeneous composition of the synthesized CDs/h-MnO2 composite. Among them, the triangular portion of the C element comes from the skeleton of the microgrid copper mesh.

Zeta potentials and contact angles were measured to characterize the surface properties of the particles. Fig. 1h displays the zeta potential variation at different stages of the synthesis process. Initially, the zeta potentials of the synthesized CDs and h-MnO2 are − 1.0 and − 56.0 mV, respectively. Subsequently, the h-MnO2 is modified with –NH2, resulting in a positive charged surface and a zeta potential of 16.6 mV for h-MnO2-A. After the CDs are combined with h-MnO2-A, a significant reduction in zeta potential of the CDs/h-MnO2-1 composite is observed, similar to that of the CDs. This finding suggests that the surface of the composite material is predominantly occupied by CDs, which is consistent with the densely distributed CDs observed in Fig. 1e. However, the CDs/h-MnO2-2 sample which was prepared without the amino groups and the EDC/NHS shows a zeta potential value of − 37.9 mV (Fig. S4), which is only a litter smaller than that of h-MnO2, means that the surface of h-MnO2 has not been fully covered by CDs in CDs/h-MnO2-2. Therefore, CDs/h-MnO2-2 exhibits a more negative potential than CDs/h-MnO2-1 due to the dominated –OH on the surface. As shown in Fig. S5, the contact angles of h-MnO2 and CDs/h-MnO2-1 are measured to be 12.2° and 10.6°, respectively. This suggests that after the modification of CDs with hydrophilic functional groups, the hydrophilicity of the composite has been well preserved. The above characterization demonstrates that CDs have been successfully loaded onto h-MnO2.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2 undergo slight weight losses below 100 °C, primarily due to the evaporation of water molecules. The weight losses of CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2 are 36.07% and 16.37% respectively until 750 °C, attributed to the decomposition of organic functional groups and the detachment of CDs from the surface of h-MnO2 [34]. In the temperature range of 100 ∼ 750 °C, the greater weight loss (Fig. S6) observed in CDs/h-MnO2-1 compared with that of h-MnO2-A could be attributed to the weight of CDs. [35,36]. Thus, the content of CDs in CDs/h-MnO2-1 is 9.34 wt%. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) results (Fig. 2a inset) show a substantially more pronounced heat absorption peak at 176 °C for CDs/h-MnO2-1, providing compelling evidence for the stronger interactions between CDs and h-MnO2 in CDs/h-MnO2-1 than in CDs/h-MnO2-2. The FTIR results depicted in Fig. 2b reveal that all the materials exhibit a characteristic absorption peak at 3425 cm−1, corresponding to the O-H stretching vibration. Additionally, the absorption peaks observed in CDs at 3199, 1706, 1614, and 1410 cm−1 are attributed to the N-H, C=O, C=C, and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [23]. Likewise, the absorption peaks of h-MnO2-A are found at 2928, 1126, 1037, and 510 cm−1, associated with the stretching vibrations of C-H, Si-OH, Si-O-C, and Mn-O, confirming the successful grafting of APTES onto h-MnO2 [37,38]. Comparatively, CDs/h-MnO2-1 shows absorption peaks at 1713 cm−1 (C=O), 1567 cm−1 (C-N-H), and 1280 cm−1 (mixed peak of C-N and N-H), corresponding to the characteristic I, II, and III peaks of the amide bonds [39]. However, CDs/h-MnO2-2 demonstrates solely O-H bending vibrations, specifically within the spectral range of 1200 ∼1800 cm−1, without distinguishable peaks associated with amide bonds. Thus, the amide bonds exist only in CDs/h-MnO2-1 but not in CDs/h-MnO2-2, also suggesting that the modification of amino groups and the EDC/NHS play key roles in the amide bonds formation process.

The XPS survey spectra of the series of samples are shown in Fig. 2c. The CDs reveal three distinct peaks at energy positions of 531.82, 399.53, and 285.10 eV, corresponding to the O1s, N1s, and C1s peaks, respectively [30]. The peak in 640 ∼ 660 eV is attributed to Mn 2p signals in h-MnO2-A [29], and the N 1s and C 1s peaks originate from the modified amino groups. Both CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2 show the characteristic peaks of the above chemical groups. The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS peaks and the corresponding designations are shown in Fig. 2d and 2e. The O1s spectrum of CDs exhibits peaks at 531.25 and 532.84 eV, belonging to the C=O and C-O functional groups [40,41]. The h-MnO2-A displays characteristic peaks at 399.31 and 400.01 eV [42,43], which suggest the presence of N-H and C-N groups resulting from the modification of the amino group. The peaks at 531.22, 532.86, 399.11, and 399.71 eV in CDs/h-MnO2-1 correspond to the C=O, C-O, N-H, and C-N groups, respectively. The area calculation reveals that the C-O, N-H, and C-N content in CDs, h-MnO2-A, CDs/h-MnO2-1, and CDs/h-MnO2-2 are as follows respectively: 19.4%, 26.2%, 15.6%, and 16.9% for C-O; 30.4%, 40.2%, 26.2%, and 32.7% for N-H; 69.6%, 59.8%, 73.8%, and 67.3% for C-N. One can observe a decrease in the C-O and N-H bonds, and an increase in the C-N content in CDs/h-MnO2-1, compared to the CDs and h-MnO2-A. The changing tendency is in line with the condensation reaction, where the C-O bond of the carboxyl group and the N-H bond of the amino group undergo cleavage to establish a new amide bonding. On the other hand, there is no such tendency in CDs/h-MnO2-2. Generally, the presence of functional groups (–COOH, –OH, and –NH2) on CDs allows for interaction with –OH on h-MnO2, forming hydrogen bonds [44,45]. These hydrogen bonds are regarded as the common interactions in both CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2. The above results confirm the formation of amide bonds (–CONH-) between CDs and h-MnO2-A in CDs/h-MnO2-1.

The composites were drop-casted onto a glass plate to prepare a thin film for optical response investigation. As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, the color of the CDs/h-MnO2-1 film changes from purple to blue at the viewing angles from 0° to 90°, with the reflectance spectra of the film showing a redshift tendency (Fig. 3c). Unlike the h-MnO2 films where the structural color is independent of the angle [29], the structural color of CDs/h-MnO2-1 film is angle-dependent. The angle-independent structural color generally originates from the amorphous photonic crystals with only short-range order [22,46]. Thus, the angle-dependent structural colors of CDs/h-MnO2-1 can be attributed to the increased orderliness during the assembly process. In contrast, the CDs/h-MnO2-2 film does not show any structural color but exhibits a dark-brown color coming from the CDs, with no obvious reflectance peaks (Fig. 3d). We depict the assembly diagrams of the two composite systems in Fig. S7 according to the TEM images of CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2. Due to the uniform coverage of CDs with multiple functional groups on the surface of CDs/h-MnO2-1, a more ordered arrangement of the film is obtained. However, the CDs exhibit an uneven distribution on the surface of CDs/h-MnO2-2 because no linking agents was added, resulting in the breaking of the orderliness and destructing of the structural color.

Schematic illustration of water-triggered reversible dual-mode response in fluorescence and structural color for CDs/h-MnO2-1 film is shown in Fig. 4 a. A small amount of water (20 µL per time) was applied to the film and its structural color/fluorescence performance under natural light/365 nm lamps in both dry and wet states were investigated. The results demonstrate that the reflectance spectrum of CDs/h-MnO2-1 shifts towards the longer wavelength by 81 nm in the wet state in comparison to the dry state, which is consistent with the observed change in structural color as illustrated in the inset (Fig. 4b). According to the Bragg formula [47,48]:
(1)
m λ = 2 n d sin θ
where m represents the number of diffraction levels, λ denotes the wavelength of the incident light, n stands for the average refractive index, d represents the spacing between planes in the lattice, and θ represents the angle between the incident light and the diffracted crystal plane, increasing of the refractive index will certainly cause a redshift of the wavelength provided that other conditions do not change. The wavelength redshift under water treatment of CDs/h-MnO2-1 is larger than that of h-MnO2 (32 nm, shown in Fig. S8), probably because the grafting of CDs increases the refractive index of CDs/h-MnO2-1. Furthermore, the surface of CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups (–NH2, –OH, and –COOH), and the introduction of CDs increases the hydrophilicity of h-MnO2 (Fig. S5). Upon water stimulation, hydrophilic groups can interact with the surrounding water molecules to form a hydrogen bonding network. The increase in the number of hydrogen bonds can significantly reduce the free volume of the system within a certain range [49,50]. According to the Lorentz-Lorenz formula [50]:
(2)
n =
1 + 2
R
/ V
1 -
R
/ V
where n stands for the average refractive index, [R] represents the molar refraction, V denotes the molecular volume (including van der Waals volume and free volume). The reduction in the free volume leads to an elevation in the refractive index of CDs/h-MnO2-1. Consequently, when exposed to water, the refractive index of CDs/h-MnO2-1 increases to a greater extent than that of h-MnO2, resulting in a greater reflectance wavelength shift of CDs/h-MnO2-1 than that of h-MnO2 in the dry/wet states. Fig. 4c presents the excellent reversibility of the reflectance spectra of the CDs/h-MnO2-1 film under 10 dry/wet cycles, consisting with the original h-MnO2 film (Fig. S9). We also dropped the same amount of water onto the CDs/h-MnO2-2 film. However, no color or the spectra change can be observed (Fig. S10), confirming that the structural color has been obscured.

The water-responsive fluorescence properties were subsequently evaluated. It is observed that both CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2 films exhibit no fluorescence in the dry state, but display intense blue fluorescence once being wetted (Fig. 4d and 4e). Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of the original CDs suspension and the composites (Fig. S11) prove that the optical properties of CDs have been well preserved after combination with h-MnO2. The fluorescence properties recorded in 10 dry/wet cycles (Fig. 4f and 4g) display that the fluorescence peak position of the two composites can be well maintained. However, the fluorescence intensities of CDs/h-MnO2-1 can maintain at 56.6% of the original value while CDs/h-MnO2-2 only remains at 8.4%. Thereby, it is recognized that the reversibility of CDs/h-MnO2-1 throughout the repeated cycles is higher than that of CDs/h-MnO2-2. Based on the above results, the CDs/h-MnO2-1 composite is anticipated to find applications in sensing, encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and related fields due to its reversible hydrochromic properties in both structural color and fluorescence.

The distinct water response reversibility of CDs/h-MnO2-1 and CDs/h-MnO2-2 is attributed to the different bonding modes. As a result of the condensation reaction between the amine groups modified on the surface of h-MnO2 and the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, amide bonds can be formed (Fig. 5 a). In the presence of both amide bonds and hydrogen bonds, CDs/h-MnO2-1 exhibits fluorescence quenching in the aggregated state in dry condition due to excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interactions [51,52]. The presence of water molecules disrupts the hydrogen bonds while leaving the amide bonds unaffected. Consequently, the CDs remain attached to h-MnO2-A, causing them to transform from the aggregated state to a finitely dispersed state and restoring their fluorescence. The re-aggregation of CDs upon evaporation of water leads to quenching of the fluorescence. In contrast, the CDs convert from an aggregated state to a fully dispersed state in CDs/h-MnO2-2 after the disruption of hydrogen bonds by water. The disordered aggregation and dispersion of CDs throughout the evaporation and re-wetting process finally leads to the escape of the CDs from their original positions. As a result, the displacement of CDs intensifies with the increased number of dry/wet cycles, and the fluorescence gradually diminishes (Fig. 5b). In a word, the amide bonds effectively anchor the CDs on the surface of h-MnO2, inducing a reversible transition between the finitely dispersed and aggregated states, thus achieving improved water-responsive reversibility in CDs/h-MnO2-1 composite compared to CDs/h-MnO2-2.

Dual-mode anti-counterfeiting models in fluorescence and structural color were assembled based on the water-triggered reversible response of CDs/h-MnO2-1. Different samples were dropped onto different areas of a glass substrate by masking, and then the solvent was evaporated. Fig. 6 a depicts a schematic diagram of a water-responsive anticounterfeiting system. We filled the Swan pattern on the left with CDs/h-MnO2-1 while filling the Swan on the right with h-MnO2 given their different response characteristics in terms of structural color and fluorescence. Fig. 6b shows the digital images. The Swan pattern exhibits vibrant blue and purple structural colors corresponding to different samples, thereby representing the initial optical state I under dry and natural light conditions. There is no observable fluorescence under 365 nm, indicating the optical state II. Both Swans exhibit a change in structural color upon wetting, displaying green (left) and blue (right) colors as the optical state III. Subsequently, the wetted Swans are illuminated by a 365 nm lamp, and only the left Swan shows blue fluorescence while the right Swan remains non-fluorescent, exemplifying the optical state IV. Ultimately, the film reverts to its original state after the evaporation of water. The above optical states I, II, III, and IV are what we call the four-levels of anti-counterfeiting. Every optical state can function as a unique medium for genuine information transmission. Therefore, the dual-mode water-triggered composite composed of h-MnO2 and CDs has great potential for applications closely related to information expression and transmission, such as anti-counterfeiting, encryption, and sensing.

In summary, we have prepared a CDs/h-MnO2 composite which features remarkable dynamic response properties of structural color and fluorescence under water stimulation. TEM, XRD, and zeta potential analyses have proved the synthesis of the composites. The formation of amide bonds (–CONH-) between the two components has been validated through FTIR, XPS, and TGA characterizations. The fluorescence reversibility of the composite CDs/h-MnO2-1 (with amide bonds and hydrogen bonds) and the control sample CDs/h-MnO2-2 (with hydrogen bonds) differs considerably, that CDs/h-MnO2-1 exhibits high stability after undergoing 10 successive water responses while the CDs/h-MnO2-2 shows poor stability. The primary reason can be attributed to the fact that upon the introduction of water molecules, the hydrogen bonding in CDs/h-MnO2-2 was disrupted, thereby releasing the carbon dots. Conversely, the amide bonding in CDs/h-MnO2-1 remained intact, effectively anchoring the carbon dots and preventing their escape. The water-responsive properties of fluorescence and structural color exhibited by CDs/h-MnO2-1 have been demonstrated to be effective in enhancing the anti-counterfeiting security levels and the information-carrying capacities. This work not only provides a non-toxic, effective, and low-cost optical response material with multiple signal channels but also contributes a path of interaction manipulation for high-performance optical security and encryption systems.

Sisi Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yajing Cai: Investigation, Data curation. Jiaying Situ: Investigation, Data curation. Biwen Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Beichen Ying: Investigation, Data curation. Jin He: Investigation, Data curation. Zhao-Qing Liu: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Jianle Zhuang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Yibo Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Basic and Applied Basic Research Program of Guangzhou Science and Technology Bureau (No. 2023A03J0076), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010152), and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A0505060007).

Liu, A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 312–318. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201503380.
